text: Describe the board's oversight of climate-related risks and opportunities. Include: board's process for being informed about climate issues, frequency of climate review, board-committee charter that includes climate, integration with strategy/budget/risk reviews.

Describe management's role in assessing and managing climate-related risks and opportunities. Include: management positions/committees with climate responsibility, climate competency requirements, links to remuneration.

Describe the climate-related risks and opportunities the organization has identified over short, medium, and long terms. Categorize as TRANSITION (policy/legal, technology, market, reputation) or PHYSICAL (acute, chronic).

Describe the impact of climate-related risks and opportunities on the organization's businesses, strategy, and financial planning. Should cover products/services, supply chain, value chain, R&D investments, operations.

Describe the resilience of the organization's strategy, taking into consideration different climate-related scenarios, including a 2°C or lower scenario. Should be quantitative where possible, ideally 1.5°C-aligned (NGFS/IPCC SR1.5 scenarios).
Describe the organization's processes for IDENTIFYING and ASSESSING climate-related risks. Should cover process for prioritization, frequency of update, geographic scope.
Describe the organization's processes for MANAGING climate-related risks. Should cover risk response (mitigate/transfer/accept/avoid), integration with enterprise risk management.
Describe how processes for identifying, assessing, and managing climate-related risks are INTEGRATED into the organization's overall risk management.
Disclose the metrics used by the organization to assess climate-related risks and opportunities in line with its strategy and risk management process. Industry-specific metrics where appropriate.
Disclose Scope 1, Scope 2, and, if appropriate, Scope 3 GHG emissions, and the related risks. Use GHG Protocol Corporate Standard. Verify with third-party assurance.
Describe the targets used by the organization to manage climate-related risks and opportunities and performance against targets. Include: scope, base year, time frames, key performance indicators.

An entity shall disclose its absolute gross Scope 1, Scope 2, and Scope 3 greenhouse gas emissions for the reporting period, measured in accordance with the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Standard.

For Scope 3 emissions, an entity shall disclose the categories included within its measure of Scope 3 emissions, in accordance with the Scope 3 categories described in the Greenhouse Gas Protocol Corporate Value Chain (Scope 3) Accounting and Reporting Standard.

An entity shall disclose its climate-related targets, including any greenhouse gas emissions targets, set by the entity to monitor progress towards achieving its strategic goals. Targets shall include: scope, time frame, base period, milestones, methodology, alignment with Paris Agreement.

E1-1: Transition plan for climate change mitigation. Entity shall disclose its transition plan to ensure compatibility with limiting global warming to 1.5°C aligned with the Paris Agreement and the climate-neutrality objective.

E1-6: Gross Scopes 1, 2, 3 and Total GHG emissions. Entity shall disclose its absolute Scope 1, 2 (location-based AND market-based), and 3 emissions across all 15 categories, with explicit listing of any excluded categories AND justification.

SBTi requires: (a) near-term target covering at least 95% of Scope 1+2 emissions, 5-10 year horizon from base year, aligned with 1.5°C trajectory; (b) Scope 3 target if Scope 3 ≥ 40% of total emissions, covering at least 67% of Scope 3; (c) net-zero target by 2050, with residual ≤ 10% offset.

Scope 3 has 15 categories: 1. Purchased goods & services; 2. Capital goods; 3. Fuel- and energy-related activities; 4. Upstream transportation & distribution; 5. Waste in operations; 6. Business travel; 7. Employee commuting; 8. Upstream leased assets; 9. Downstream transport & distribution; 10. Processing of sold products; 11. Use of sold products; 12. End-of-life treatment of sold products; 13. Downstream leased assets; 14. Franchises; 15. Investments.
